title: 第 3 節
en: Relation between the Vedic and other Aryan Mythologies.

It has been questioned whether the mythology of the Veda is really so intimately connected with that of other Aryan nations as its language has been proved to be, and whether it is more primitive than all the rest. Here again we must try to come to a clear understanding. That the Aryan mythologies spring from a common source, the one equation of Dyaush-pitar, Zevs naT-rjp, and Ju-piter has placed once for all beyond the reach of reasonable doubt. And when we say that Vedic mythology represents a period of thought which had almost entirely passed away before we can conceive the possibility of the Olympian Pantheon, or of the mythological creations of Italy and Germany, we do not speak simply of 452 VEDIC AND OTHER ARYAN MYTHOLOGIES. [chap.

chronological priority. What chronology is there that could settle the dates of the birth and the death of the Aryan gods ? Some myths die soon and are forgotten, while others possess so peculiar a vitality that they survive from age to age in all their freshness and youthfulness, that is, in their intelligibility, or what has been called their transparency. If the Vedic mythology, such as it is, could be proved to have sprung up but yesterday in a desert island or in the moon, its psychological priority and its psychological interest would remain just the same.
Still, however simple in its conceptions Vedic mythology may be, as compared to Greek and Roman mythology, it would not be right to claim for it a primordial character, whatever that may mean.

More Modern Ingredients in Vedic Mythology. Even the oldest Vedic hymns and the oldest Vedic gods often exhibit a decidedly secondary and tertiary character. As we find here and there in Greek, Latin, or Lituanian grammatical forms more primitive than their corresponding forms in Sanskrit, we find in their mythologies also some names and legends less disturbed than what corresponds to them in the Veda. This has always been admitted by all serious scholars.

Ceremonial Ideas in Vedic Hymns. But some of them seem to me to have gone a great deal too far in maintaining that all Vedic hymns are productions of a modern or secondary age, compositions in fact of priests, and solely intended as accompaniments to a highly developed Vl] CEREMONIAL IDEAS IN VEDIC HYMNS. 453 sacrificial ceremonial. The reason of this misconception is that some scholars have formed to themselves a kind of ideal of what primitive poetry ought to be, and starting from this ideal, which has no existence except in their brain, of a pre-adamite or half-angelic childhood of the human race, they declare even the most ancient poems as time-worn and effete. The question is, do we know any Aryan poetry less time-worn and more ancient than that of the Veda ? That a considerable portion of our Vedic hymns was preserved simply because they could be fitted into the sacrificial framework of the Brahmans, no Vedic scholar has ever doubted. But this is very different from asserting that we possess no Vedic poetry, nay that no Vedic poetry existed before the institution of the Vedic ceremonial.
Sacrifice and Prayer.

Some theorists went even further and maintained that by some inw^ard necessity sacrifice everywhere comes first and sacred poetry second. Such general assertions carry little weight. All depends here on what we mean by sacrifice. If, as the Vedic poet says, it is a sacrifice to Agni, the god of fire, to put but one log of wood on the hearth l, if to bend the knee before the rising sun, or to lift the hands towards heaven during a thunderstorm is to be called worship, then it would certainly be difficult to say which comes first, praise or worship. But if sacrifice means what the Brahmans meant by their Ya^fia, a most complicated ceremonial, though presupposed even in many of the hymns, then to suppose that no 1 A past. Sutras II, 9, 21.
454 SACRIFICE AND PRAYER. [chap.

word of prayer or praise did ever proceed in measured cadence from the lips of the Brahmans, till they had elaborated their minute sacrificial ceremonial, their &rauta sacrifices, is opposed to all we know, whether from psychology or from history. Who is competent to settle who came first, the priest or the poet, if not the priest himself ? Yet the author of Hymn 88 in the tenth Ma^c/ala, whether we call him priest or poet, says, Rv. X, 88, 8 : — Siiktavakam prathamdm at it agnim, At it haviA a^anayanta deva/i. ' The gods created first the reciter of hymns, then Agni (the sacrificial fire), then the sacrificial offerings.'

This looks almost like an intentional protest against the opposite theory ; at all events, it cannot be treated as a merely casual and unimportant remark. There are in the Rig-veda hymns utterly inappropriate for sacrificial purposes, and there are other hymns that had actually to be altered in some minute points (uha) in order to suit the requirements of a complicated liturgy. If former students of the Veda neglected the liturgical elements, those who have since called attention to their existence and importance have, as usual, allowed themselves to be carried away much too far in the opposite direction. It is true, no doubt, that in very early times the sacrifice has taken a larger development in India than anywhere else. Only we should be careful. If we are told that karman must be translated by sacrifice, this is true ; but the reason is that sacrifice in early times was simply any act that accompanied a prayer. Nor must we imagine, because we translate yar/wa, kratu, adhvara by Vl] SACRIFICE AND PRAYER. 455 sacrifice, that therefore the ancient sacrifices in India were the same thing as the sacrifices of which we read among the Jews, Greeks, and Romans, or, it may be, the sacrifices and orgies of uncivilised and savage races. The growth of sacrificial customs has to be studied separately in every country. If we found the minute sacrificial rules which have been preserved to us in the Brahma?ias and Sutras in India, repeated in Greece and Rome, or even in the canons of the Christian Church, we should say at once that such rules could only be the result of a long continued, and therefore of a far advanced and very modern civilisation, and that they prove the existence of a long established and powerful priesthood. I pointed out many years ago, 1859, in my History of Ancient Sanskrit Literature, p. 493, how perplexing it was to see the most minute ceremonial distinctions recognised not only in the Brahmawas and Sutras, but already in the hymns of the Rig-veda. I showed how the names of the seven different classes of priests, intended each for their own special sacrificial acts, such as HotW, Potri, NesbiW, Agnidh, PrasastW, Adhvaryu, and Brahman, occurred in the hymns, and had become so familiar that some of them had actually been transferred to Agni, the god of the sacrificial fire. I have nothing to retract from what I said then, and I can only repeat that, in order to account for the apparently artificial and modern ideas occurring in these hymns, I should give anything to be able to assign to the final collection of the hymns of the Rig-veda a much later date than what is commonly assigned to it. But I see as yet no chance of escape from my own arguments, and I often wish that those who repeat 456 SACRIFICE AND PRAYER. [chap.

ao-ain and ao-ain that Vedic literature has been proved to be much more modern than I supposed it to be, would give me their proofs. No one would be more grateful for them than I. But as the case stands at present, I am afraid that nothing remains but to try to learn a new lesson, namely that religious acts, and even minute ceremonial rules and distinctions, may under favouring circumstances spring up in a much earlier stage of civilisation than we were led to suppose from a study of the religion of the Jews, the Greeks and Romans, or even of savage races. At all events, the fact that many of the Yedic hymns presuppose on the part of the poets a familiarity with senseless ceremonial minutiae, cannot possibly justify the historian of religion in maintaining that in India ceremonial came first, and sacred poetry second, still less, as has been asserted by some writers who are not Yedic scholars, that there are no hymns in the Rig-veda which were not from the first composed by priests, and exclusively for sacrificial purposes. One cannot well prove what stands written on so many pages of the Rig-veda. If we call every petition addressed to the Devas, every praise of their power and majesty, which was afterwards employed in the course of certain sacrifices, a sacrificial hymn, then no doubt the majority of the poems collected in the Rig-veda may go by that name. If we imagine that no gift of water, honey, milk, butter, or cake could be made to the gods except by a priest, or that every libation at the beginning or end of a meal, every surrender of a valued possession on the funeral pile or on a fresh grave, is a, sacrifice, then most of the hymns of the Vedic ifoshis, though even then by no means Vi] SACRIFICE AND PRAYER, 457 all, may be called mere accessories of sacrificial performances. But there are surely recurrent, and more or less solemn acts in the morning and the evening, if consisting only in the stirring and lighting of the fire on the hearth in the morning, and its careful making up in the evening, there is the glory of the full moon, and the return of the new moon which call forth religious feelings, and become an excuse for more or less boisterous festivities. There was even a definite practical purpose in the celebration of the return of the seasons, whether three or four or five or six, in the observation of the four stations of the sun at the time of the shortest and the longest days, and, later on, at the vernal and autumnal equinoxes. All this is natural, and indispensable, simply for the sake of keeping time, though it does not presuppose a priesthood or a ceremonial canon. I feel more and more convinced that the true origin of the solemn public sacrifices must be sought for in the necessity of establishing and maintaining a kind of calendar for reckoning days, sennights, fortnights, months, seasons, and years. This would naturally lead to the establishment of festivals on certain days of the year, but these need not have been more at first than gatherings and feastings of families, clans, or villages, with praises of certain gods who are more intimately connected with certain days or seasons, and with friendly recollections of departed members of the family. But when we meet with a fixed number of priests and their various offices, an elaborate construction of the altars intended for various fires, a large number of sacrificial implements, and the preparation of every kind of sacrificial 4=-8 SACRIFICE AND PRAYER. [chap.

offering, we feel no doubt that we have reached a new and comparatively modern stage. Only need we suppose that such a stage was never reached, before some poetical expression had been given to the motives which, we cannot doubt, inspired every one of these solemn performances ? It is quite possible that such poetical utterances had an intimate relation to such acts as the lighting of the fire in the morning and its protection in the evening ; but an expression of gratitude for the genial warmth of the fire on the hearth, or a praise of the sun at the return of spring, need surely not have waited till there was a special priest to perform the act of kindling the fire, till the logs that should be used for such a purpose had been carefully measured and numbered, &c.

I am always most reluctant to differ on questions of this kind from Grimm. Grimm (Kleinere Schriften, ii, p. 460) admits, as is well known, three successive periods, the first where people sacrificed only, the second where they sacrificed and prayed, the third where they prayed only. I do not see, however, that this was more than a postulate with Grimm, and I doubt whether there are facts to support it.
Magic and "Witchcraft.

It has been said, and I think truly said, that much of the Vedic ceremonial savours of magic and witchcraft, and bears a certain likeness to the sorcery and black art practised by savage races in ancient and modern times. It could hardly be otherwise, for every sacrifice contains the germs of magic within it. Sacrifices even of the simplest Vl] MAGIC AND WITCHCRAFT. 459 kind were supposed to produce an effect indirectly through the intervention of divine beings. This too may be called superstitious, but at the time and to the persons most concerned, it was not without a certain logic. Believing in gods as they did, they naturally believed that their gods were accessible to the same arguments and the same bribes which produced an effect on their friends on earth. As soon, however, as these Devas were left out of consideration, sacrifice and prayer by themselves were supposed to exercise a direct and constraining influence on nature in producing the events that were desired. They may then be said to have assumed a magical character. It is difficult to draw a sharp line of demarcation between these two kinds of ceremonies, and between the psychological dispositions that gave rise to them. The very words we use are ill-defined. In German the word commonly used for magic is Zauber, but it is by no means easy to say under what circumstances Zauber comprehends sorcery, witchcraft, enchantment, and black art. If a priest implores the help of the god of rain, or offers presents to Pan/anya, this would probably be called a sacrifice, but if by his spells and contortions he pretends to force par^anya, the raincloud, to send down rain, it would seem at once to become magic or Zauber1. Still there is clearly a transition between the two. Magic, in fact, seems to stand to sacrifice in the same relation in which superstition stands to faith, or logos to myth, and it may be stated without fear of contradiction, that every sacrificial act, the original purpose of which 1 Oldenberg, 1. c, pp. 435, 448, 459 seq. VOL. II. D 460 MAGIC AND WITCHCRAFT. [CHAP.

has been forgotten, is very apt to become magical. Or if this seems to be too wide a generalisation, we may say at all events that every magical act which now seems irrational, must originally have had a reason, and that it is the highest object of our studies to discover that reason.
Von Ihering's Rationalism.

In this respect Prof. Ihering has set us a splendid example to follow, showing us how there must be and how there is reason at the bottom of everything, however unreasonable it seems to us, in the customs and laws of the ancient world. What could seem more magical than the auguria taken by an army on its march ? Why did they throw grain before the fowls and watch their movements ? Because originally, as Prof. Ihering has shown, when entering into an unknown country, it was often a question of life and death whether the grain and berries that were found growing wild were poisonous or wholesome. The only way to find this out was to throw some of these grains before the fowls which had been brought on purpose, and to watch the result. As soon as the real purpose was forgotten, this augurium became a purely magical art, kept up by the augurs for their own purposes, but deprived of its original purpose (Zweck). We are told that in some of the islands of the Southern Sea the natives will eat no berries unless some of them have been pecked by the birds.

It is clear that such acts, which had dwindled down to mere superstitions, are far more likely to receive some kind of explanation in countries which possess an ancient literature or tradition vi] von iiiering's rationalism. 461 than among African or Australian savages, where we never get beyond the surface of the day. In India, in Greece, in Italy we may catch a glimpse now and then through the twilight of a more or less continuous tradition ; in Africa and Australia we move in complete darkness. It is much more likely, therefore, that a study of such works as the Atharvaveda, the Kausika-sutras, the Adbhuta-brahmana, the GWhya-sutras, &c, may help us to understand the magical practices of Zulus and Red Indians than vice versa, and nothing can be more useful to the anthropologist by profession than a careful study of the Vedic age, with its prayers, its sacrifices, its imprecations and superstitions, if he wishes to understand some of the secret springs of magic and witchcraft as practised by Red Indians or Maoris. A study of the >Sraddha sacrifices, of the cult of the PitWs in India, and of the Fravashis of Persia, has helped us to understand for the first time the true meaning of ancestor-worship, so widely spread among civilised and uncivilised races of the present day ; why should not more light come from the same quarter in elucidation of Shamanism or Hwang-kiao ?
Atharva-veda.

I know there are many scholars who would protest against studying what they mean by Vedic Religion in such works as the Atharva-veda, the Kausika-sutras, or the Adbhuta-brahma?ia, and it is generally agreed that the Atharva-veda is very modern as compared to the Rig-veda. Even those who stand up for the antiquity of the Atharva hymns, would probably admit that they belong to a totally different stratum from that which gave D 2 462 ATHARVA-VEDA. [chap.

rise to the hymns of the Big-veda. Still we know of nothing nearer to the other Vedas than the Atharva-veda, and though most scholars would agree that the Atharva-veda should at first be studied by itself, and not be mixed up with the other Vedas, many of the superstitions and purely magical mantras of the Atharvangirasas can only be explained by a reference to the Big-veda, and not by consulting the accounts of travellers among the tribes of Northern Asia and Northern America.
Mordvinian and Wotjakian Sacrifices.

But though I am not very sanguine of our ever gaining complete light on the origin of sacrificial and magical performances from the lowest of savages, from Bushmen or Australians, I have always been ready to learn a lesson from the ceremonial customs of such races as Mordvines and Wotjakes, who possess something like trustworthy traditions of the past, and whose language has been carefully studied by real scholars. We all know how precarious and how perplexing the study of ancient customs is, how rarely we can find competent and trustworthy authorities, and how almost impossible it is to get any historical information extending beyond the present generation, and laying bare the roots from which certain customs have sprung. The exjDlanation given by a native is generally considered as settling such matters, but it is only too well known by those who have trusted to that kind of evidence, how readily a native supplies whatever is wanted, and how innocently he tenders his own private impressions as representing the opinion of a whole clan or a whole people.
vi] SOCIETE FINNOOUGKIENNE. 463 Soci6t6 Finno-Ougrienne.

We ought to be all the more grateful, therefore, to the Societe Finno-Ougrienne for having sent from time to time competent scholars to report not only on the poetry but likewise on the customs, sacrificial or otherwise, of races still in a transition state, and without a sacred literature of their own, and, even when acquainted with and partially converted to Christianity, still performing their pagan sacrifices and worshipping their pagan deities. Such are, for instance, the Mordvinians, scattered in the district of Astrakhan on the upper course of the Volga, as lately described by M. W. Mainof in the fifth volume of the Journal de la Societe FinnoOugrienne, 1889. Such are the Erzjanians, a branch of the Mordvinians, whose prayers, riddles, and incantations have been studied by M. H. Paasonen in the twelfth volume of the same Journal, 1894. And such are the Wotjakians, specimens of whose popular and sacred poetry have been collected and published by M. Yrjo Wichmann in the eleventh volume, 1893. These Wotjakians are chiefly found in the governments of Wjatka, Kasan, and Perm.

Among such races, if anywhere, we may hope to learn something of the true origin of sacrificial acts and sacrificial poetry and of the mutual relation of the two. The authorities which I shall quote are all Finno-Ugrian scholars, and in their hands we may feel safe against such blunders and misunderstandings as are but too common in the works of casual travellers, and missionaries, even if stationed for years in the same places. Anyhow it will be seen that I have not neglected to study collateral 464 SOCIETE FINNO-OUGRIENNE. [chap.
evidence, even if taken from non-Aryan races, if only the authorities we have to follow are trustworthy and vouched for by so eminent a body as the Societe Finno-Ougrienne.

We found before how the mythology of these Finno-Ugrian races offered striking analogies to the mythology of the Veda, and it is possible, therefore, that their sacrificial customs also may exhibit some useful parallels to the early ceremonial of the Vedic priesthood.
zh: 吠陀神話學與其他雅利安神話學之間的關係。

有人質疑吠陀的神話是否真的如其語言已被證明的那樣，與其他雅利安民族的神話有如此密切的關聯；同時也質疑它是否比其他一切神話都更為原始。在此我們必須再次力求達成清晰的理解。雅利安諸神話源於同一淵源這一事實，已由特尤斯‧皮塔爾（Dyaush-pitar）、宙斯‧帕特爾（Zevs patēr）與朱庇特（Ju-piter）的同源關係永遠地排除了合理懷疑的餘地。而當我們說吠陀神話代表了一個思想時期，該時期在我們能夠想像奧林匹斯萬神殿或意大利與德國的神話創作成為可能之前，幾乎已經完全消逝之際，我們所言並非僅是
時間的優先性。究竟有什麼樣的編年學能夠確定雅利安諸神的誕生與死亡之日期呢？有些神話很快就消亡被人遺忘，而另有些神話卻擁有如此獨特的生命力，得以代代相傳而歷久彌新，亦即保持其可理解性，或者說保持其所謂的「透明性」（transparency）。倘若梨俱吠陀神話，其本貌如此，竟可被證明只是昨日才在某座荒漠孤島或月球上產生，其心理學上的優先性及其心理學上的意義仍將保持不變。
儘管吠陀神話在其觀念上相比希臘與羅馬神話可能顯得簡樸，但聲稱它具有原初性質（無論這究竟意味著什麼）仍然是不恰當的。
即使是最古老的吠陀讚歌及其最古老的提婆，往往呈現出明顯的次級和三級特徵。正如我們在希臘文、拉丁文或立陶宛文的語法形式中時或發現比梵文相應形式更為原始的形態，我們在其神話中也發現了某些名稱和傳說，比吠陀中相應的內容受到更少的擾亂。所有認真的學者向來都承認此一事實。

然而其中有些學者在主張所有吠陀讚歌都是現代或次級時代的作品、實際上是祭司的創作，且純粹意在作為高度發展的祭祀儀式的附隨物時，似乎走得太遠了。這種誤解的原因在於某些學者為自己構想了一種原始詩歌應當具備的理想樣態，並從這一純粹存在於其腦海中、毫無現實基礎的理想出發——這個理想涉及人類種族亞當前或半天使般的幼年時期——他們甚至將最古老的詩歌也宣稱為飽經風霜、業已衰落。問題是，我們是否認識任何比吠陀更不陳舊、更為古老的雅利安詩歌？沒有任何吠陀學者曾經懷疑，我們相當一部分的吠陀讚歌之所以得以保存，實在是因為它們能被納入婆羅門的祭儀框架之中。但這與聲稱我們不擁有任何吠陀詩歌、甚至沒有任何吠陀詩歌存在於吠陀祭儀制度建立之前，是有很大區別的。
祭祀與祈禱

有些理論家甚至走得更遠，主張基於某種內在的必然性，祭祀在任何地方都先於神聖詩歌而出現。這類籠統的主張分量不足。這裡一切取決於我們對「祭祀」（sacrifice）一詞的理解。倘若如吠陀詩人所說，向火神阿耆尼（Agni）獻祭不過是在爐火上放一根木頭¹，或者向上升的太陽屈膝禮拜，或在雷電交加時舉起雙手朝向蒼天，都被稱為敬拜（worship），那麼說頌讚還是敬拜何者居先，便確實難以判斷。但若祭祀是指婆羅門所謂的雅吉那（Ya^ña）——一種極其複雜的儀式，儘管在許多讚歌中已有預設——那麼要假定沒有¹ 《過去經》（A past. Sutras）II, 9, 21。
454 祭祀與禱告。[章

在婆羅門人的嘴唇上發出有節奏的祈禱或讚美之詞，直到他們精心琢磨出他們的細緻祭祀儀式、他們的室魯塔祭祀（śrauta sacrifices）為止，這樣的說法與我們從心理學或歷史所知的一切都相悖。如果不是祭司自己，還有誰能夠決定祭司和詩人誰先出現呢？然而《梨俱吠陀》第十曼達拉第88首讚歌的作者，無論我們稱他為祭司還是詩人，在《梨俱吠陀》10卷88篇8節中說：——*Sukta-vakam prathamam atiragnim, Ati haviA ajanayanta devah*。「諸神首先創造了讚歌的誦唱者，其次是阿耆尼（祭祀火），其次是祭祀供物。」

這看起來幾乎像是對相反理論的有意抗議；無論如何，它不能被視為僅僅是隨意且無關緊要的評論。梨俱吠陀中有一些讚歌完全不適合祭祀用途，還有其他讚歌為了適應複雜的禮儀規範，實際上必須在某些細微之處（uha）加以改動。如果早期的吠陀研究者忽視了禮儀因素，那些後來強調其存在和重要性的人，如同往常，卻又走向了相反方向，過度極端化。誠然，在很早的時代，祭祀在印度發展出的規模確實遠超其他任何地方。只是我們應該謹慎小心。如果有人告訴我們業（karman）必須譯為祭祀，這是對的；但原因在於早期的祭祀就是任何伴隨祈禱的行為。我們也不應該想像，因為我們將 yajña、kratu、adhvara 譯為祭祀，古印度的祭祀就等同於我們在猶太人、希臘人和羅馬人的記載中讀到的祭祀，或者在未開化和野蠻民族的祭祀與狂歡中讀到的祭祀。祭祀習俗的發展必須在每個國家分別研究。如果我們在希臘和羅馬，甚至在基督教會的教規中，發現與保存在印度的婆羅門書（Brāhmanas）和經文集（Sutras）中相同的細微祭祀規則，我們應該立即說，如此規則只能是經過長期延續、因此相當發達且非常現代的文明的結果，並且它們證明了一個長期確立且強大的祭司階級的存在。早在一八五九年，我在《古代梵文文獻史》第四九三頁中曾指出，看到最細微的儀式區分不僅在婆羅門書和經文集中被認可，而且已經在梨俱吠陀的讚歌中出現，是多麼令人困惑。我曾展示七個不同祭司階級的名稱——每個階級各有其特定的祭祀行為，如侯特律（Hotṛ）、波特律（Potṛ）、涅什蒂（Neṣṭṛ）、阿格尼德（Agnidh）、普拉薩斯蒂（Prasāstṛ）、阿德瓦里尤（Adhvaryu）和婆羅門（Brahman）——如何出現在讚歌中，並變得如此熟悉，以至於其中一些甚至已被轉用於祭祀之火之神阿格尼身上。我對當時所言毫無保留，我只能重申，為了解釋這些讚歌中出現的表面上人為的和現代的觀念，我願意付出任何代價來將梨俱吠陀讚歌的最終結集時間指定為比通常所認定的時間要晚得多。但我目前看不到任何逃脫自身論證的機會，我常常希望那些重複

那些認為吠陀文獻遠比我原本所設想的更為晚近的人，如果肯提出他們的證據，我必定感激不盡。但就目前情況而言，我恐怕別無他法，只能試著吸取一個新的教訓，即宗教行為，甚至細微的禮儀規則與區別，在有利的環境下，可能產生於遠比我們從猶太人、希臘人、羅馬人，或甚至野蠻民族的宗教研究中所推想的更早期的文明階段。無論如何，許多吠陀讚歌預設詩人熟悉無意義的禮儀細節這一事實，絕不能為宗教史家辯護其主張：在印度禮儀乃是首先出現，而聖詩乃其次，更遠論某些非吠陀學者所主張的，即梨俱吠陀中並無非由祭司自始就為祭祀目的而作的讚歌。凡是寫在梨俱吠陀許多篇頁上的東西，實難以證明。若我們將每一首向提婆獻上的請求、對其權能與威嚴的讚頌，以及隨後在某些祭祀中被採用的詩歌，都稱為祭祀讚歌，那麼無疑梨俱吠陀中所收集的大多數詩篇都可被如此稱呼。若我們想象沒有祭司的參與，就不能將水、蜂蜜、牛乳、奶油或糕餅獻給諸神，或者每次在餐前或餐後的灑奠，每次在火葬堆或新墳上獻出珍貴物品的行為，都是祭祀，那麼吠陀詩人的讚歌中大部分，儘管即使如此也絕非全部，都可被稱為祭祀儀式的附屬物。但肯定有週期性的，或多或少莊嚴的晨昏之行為，即使僅僅包括早晨點燃和撥動爐火，以及晚間仔細處理爐火；滿月的榮光與新月的歸來，會喚起宗教感情，成為或多或少熱烈的慶祝活動的藉口。季節迴圈的慶祝——無論是三季、四季、五季或六季——確實有明確的實際目的，在太陽於冬至與夏至，以及稍後在春分與秋分時之四個站點的觀測中亦是如此。所有這些都是自然的，且完全不可或缺，單為了計時之目的，儘管它並不預設祭司階級或禮儀法典的存在。我越來越確信，莊嚴的公眾祭祀的真正起源必須在建立與維持某種曆法以計算天數、週、半月、月份、季節與年份的必要性中尋求。這自然而然會導致在某些年度日期建立節慶，但這些最初所需的只不過是家族、宗族或村落的聚集與盛宴，伴有對某些與特定日期或季節更密切相關之提婆的讚美，以及對已故家族成員的友善追憶。但當我們遇見固定數量的祭司及其各式職位、為各類聖火而精心構造的祭壇、大量祭祀工具，以及各種祭祀物品的準備時，

獻祭時，我們毫無疑問地感受到自己已達到一個嶄新且相對現代的階段。我們只需假設在給予這些莊嚴儀式的動機以詩意表達之前，從未到達過這樣的階段——而我們不能懷疑這些動機啟發了這些儀式的每一次執行。這樣的詩意表述很有可能與早晨點燃聖火及晚間保護聖火這類行為有著密切的關係；但對於爐火溫和熱力的感恩之辭，或對春日太陽回歸的讚頌，應該無須等待到有專門的祭司來執行點火儀式、直到應該用於此目的的木柴被仔細測量並編號等等的時候。
我總是對於這類問題與格林相悖感到萬分不願。眾所周知，格林（《較小著作集》第二卷，第四六○頁）承認三個相繼的時期：第一個時期人們只獻祭，第二個時期他們既獻祭又祈禱，第三個時期他們只祈禱。然而，我看不出這對格林而言不過是一個假設而已，我懷疑是否存在事實足以支持它。

Magic and Witchcraft, though often confounded with Religion, must be carefully distinguished from it. Magic is an attempt to force the invisible world to do the bidding of man; Religion is a submission to the will of the invisible world, and a desire to be in harmony with it. Magic proceeds from man, and is directed towards the invisible world; Religion proceeds from the invisible world, and is directed towards man. In Magic, man is the agent; in Religion, the Deity is the agent. Magic pretends to control the invisible world by the knowledge of certain words, signs, and ceremonies. The magician believes that he can compel even the Deity to obey him, if he only knows the right formula. With the help of certain charms, spells, and incantations, he thinks he can bend the will of nature to his own purposes. Magic is therefore essentially selfish; it is a struggle of man against the Deity, not a reconciliation with Him. There are degrees in Magic. The most primitive form is Fetishism, or the belief that certain material objects possess supernatural powers. Higher forms of Magic are Sorcery and Witchcraft proper, which imply a knowledge of supernatural forces, and an attempt to use them for private ends. The belief in Magic is almost universal; it is found among the most barbarous nations, and it lingers even among civilized people. The belief in Witchcraft, or the power to injure others by supernatural means, is one of the most widespread and most persistent forms of superstition. Like Magic, it must be carefully distinguished from Religion. A witch is not a priestess; she does not serve the Deity, but rather attempts to work against the Deity and against the welfare of her neighbours. Witchcraft is therefore a perversion of Religion; it is a relapse into Magic. 魔法與巫術雖然經常混淆於宗教，但必須謹慎區分。魔法是人類試圖強制無形世界順應人類之意願；宗教則是對無形世界意志的臣服，以及渴望與之和諧相處。魔法源自人類，並指向無形世界；宗教源自無形世界，並指向人類。在魔法中，人類是行動者；在宗教中，神祇才是行動者。 魔法聲稱可以藉由特定言辭、符號與儀式之知識而支配無形世界。魔法師相信，只要他知曉正確的咒語，甚至可以強制神祇服從他。藉助特定的護身符、咒語與咒文之幫助，他認為自己可以將自然之意志彎曲以就己意。因此魔法本質上是自私的；它是人類對抗神祇的鬥爭，而非與之的和解。 魔法有其等級。最原始的形式是物神崇拜（Fetishism），即相信特定物質對象具有超自然力量。更高形式的魔法是巫術與真正的巫術，它們暗示對超自然力量的知識，以及企圖將之用於私人目的。對魔法的信念幾乎是普遍的；它存在於最野蠻的民族中，甚至在文明人民中依舊流傳。 對巫術的信念，或藉由超自然手段傷害他人的能力之信念，是最廣泛與最持久的迷信形式之一。與魔法相似，它必須謹慎區分於宗教。女巫並非祭司；她不侍奉神祇，反而企圖違抗神祇與鄰人福祉。因此巫術是宗教的敗壞；它是退回魔法之中。

有人說過，而我認為這個說法是真實的，吠陀祭儀的許多成分帶有魔法和巫術的色彩，與古今野蠻民族所實踐的巫蠱與黑魔法頗為相似。這幾乎是不可避免的，因為每一種祭祀都蘊含著魔法的萌芽。即使是最簡單的祭祀，也被認為能夠通過諸神的介入而間接產生效果。這雖然也可以稱為迷信，但在當時和對於當事者而言，卻並非不具有某種邏輯性。既然他們信仰諸神，自然就相信他們的神也會受到同樣的論證和賄賂所影響，就像他們在人間對朋友施加影響一樣。然而，一旦將這些提婆撇開不顧，祭祀和禱告本身就被認為能夠直接而有力地作用於自然界，產生所欲望的事件。這樣的儀式就可以說具有了魔法的性質。在這兩類儀式之間，以及在導致它們產生的心理狀態之間，很難劃出明確的分界線。我們所使用的詞彙本身就定義不清。在德語中，通常用來表示魔法的詞是 Zauber，但要精確規定 Zauber 在什麼情況下包括巫蠱、巫術、迷幻和黑魔法，實在是困難得很。如果祭司祈求雨神的幫助，或向帕爾尼亞（Parṇaya）獻祭，這通常會被稱為祭祀；但如果祭司通過他的咒語和扭曲身體的動作，假裝強迫雨雲帕爾尼亞降雨，那便似乎立刻成為魔法或 Zauber。儘管如此，兩者之間顯然存在著過渡。事實上，魔法對於祭祀的關係，就像迷信對於信仰、或邏各斯（logos）對於神話一樣，可以毫無疑問地指出，每一種祭祀行為，其原初的目的
已被遺忘者，甚為容易流於魔術。倘若此說似乎概括過廣，我們至少可以說，凡是如今顯得不理性的每一個魔術行為，原本必然有其理由，而發現此理由乃是我們研究的最高目標。

Von Ihering, a German jurist and philosopher, has lately tried to apply rationalistic principles to the science of religion. He maintains that religion originated in fear, and that all religious conceptions are the result of ignorance and superstition. According to him, the gods are nothing but personifications of natural phenomena, created by man in order to explain what he could not otherwise understand. This theory, though plausible at first sight, does not account for the deeper meaning and the moral significance of religion. It reduces all religious thought to mere hallucination and delusion, and ignores the fact that even the most primitive religions contain elements of truth and moral wisdom. 馮‧耶林是一位德國法學家兼哲學家，近來嘗試將理性主義原則運用於宗教學之中。他主張宗教源於恐懼，所有宗教觀念皆為無知與迷信的產物。依其所見，諸神不過是人為了解釋自己無法理解的事物而創造的自然現象的人格化。此一理論乍看似乎合理，卻無法說明宗教的深層意義及其道德的重要性。它將所有宗教思想化約為純粹的幻覺與妄想，並忽視了即便最原始的宗教亦包含真理與道德智慧的要素這一事實。

在這方面，艾赫林教授為我們樹立了一個光輝的典範，向我們展示了無論多麼顯得不合理，在古代世界的風俗和法律中，一切事物的根底都必然具有理性，而且確實具有理性。還有什麼比軍隊行進中進行的鳥卜（auguria）更顯得魔法性呢？他們為何向禽類拋撒穀粒並觀察其動作？正如艾赫林教授所已證明的，原因在於：當進入一個陌生的國家時，所發現的野生穀物和漿果是否有毒或無毒，往往是生死存亡的問題。唯一查明此事的方式，就是將這些穀粒拋到為此目的特意帶來的禽類面前，並觀察結果。一旦真實的目的被遺忘，這種鳥卜就變成了純粹的魔法藝術，由鳥卜師為了自身的目的而保留下來，卻喪失了其原初的目的（Zweck）。據稱在南海的某些島嶼上，土著居民除非先有鳥類啄食過漿果，否則就不會進食。

顯然，這類已經衰退為純粹迷信的儀式，在擁有古代文獻或傳統的國家中，遠比在非洲或澳洲蠻族地區更有可能獲得某種解釋，因為在後者，我們永遠無法超越日常表面。在印度、希臘、意大利，我們或許能通過綿延不斷傳統的暮光窺見一隅；在非洲和澳洲，我們卻身處完全的黑暗中。因此，研究《雅磨吠陀》（Atharvaveda）、《考西迦經》（Kausika-sutras）、《奇異婆羅門經》（Adbhuta-brahmana）、《家庭經》（Grhya-sutras）等著作，比研究祖魯人和紅印地安人的魔法實踐更有可能相互闡發；反之亦然。對於希望理解紅印地安人或毛利人所實踐的魔法與巫術之祕密根源的職業人類學家而言，沒有什麼比仔細研究吠陀時代更有用的了——包括其禱告、祭祀、詛咒和迷信。對於《施拉德哈祭祀》（Sraddha sacrifices）、印度的《毗多神靈祭祀》（cult of the Pitris）以及波斯的《弗拉瓦什神靈》（Fravashis）的研究，使我們首次得以理解祖先崇拜的真正意義，而祖先崇拜在當今文明和未開化的民族中都廣泛流行；那麼何以不能從同一來源尋求更多光亮，以闡明薩滿教或黃教呢？
阿闥婆吠陀。
我知道有許多學者會反對在《阿闥婆吠陀》（Atharva-veda）、《考西卡經軌》（Kausika-sutras）或《奇異梵書》（Adbhuta-brahma?ia）等著作中研究他們所謂的吠陀宗教，而且普遍公認《阿闥婆吠陀》與《梨俱吠陀》相比是相當晚出的。即使是那些主張《阿闥婆讚歌》具有古老性的人，也可能會承認它們屬於一個完全不同的地層，與產生梨俱吠陀宗教內涵的地層截然有別。
升華為梨俱吠陀的讚歌。然而我們對於其他吠陀之接近程度，無以復加於阿闥婆吠陀（Atharva-veda）；雖然大多數學者同意阿闥婆吠陀起初應當獨立研究，而不應與其他吠陀混為一談，但阿闥婆吠陀祭司（Atharvangirasas）的許多迷信與純粹魔法的咒語，其解釋只能參照梨俱吠陀，而不能藉由諮詢北亞與北美洲部落民族的旅遊記述來得到說明。
莫德溫語民族與沃特亞克語民族的祭祀

不過，雖然我對於能否從最低等的野蠻人——布須曼人或澳洲人——身上完全闡明祭祀儀式和魔法儀式的起源並不抱持樂觀態度，但我始終樂於向那些擁有相當可信的傳統記載、其語言又曾經過真正學者仔細研究的民族——如莫德溫人和沃爾雅克人——學習其儀式習俗的課題。我們都深知古代習俗的研究是多麼危險而令人困惑，我們多麼難得能尋得稱職且可信的權威人士，以及要獲得任何超越當代、揭示某些習俗所由來的根源的歷史資訊是多麼幾近不可能。本地人所給予的解釋通常被視為解決了這類問題，但凡曾倚賴這類證據的人都深知，本地人往往極其樂意提供任何所需之物，而他也天真地將自己個人的印象當作整個氏族或整個民族的意見而呈獻出來。
芬蘭-烏戈爾學會

因此，我們更應當對芬烏戈爾學會（Societe Finno-Ougrienne）心存感恩，感謝該學會不時派遣能幹的學者，不僅報告這些民族的詩歌，同樣也報告其習俗——無論是祭祀性質或其他——這些民族仍處於過渡狀態，本身並無聖典文學，即便已接觸基督教並部分改宗，仍舊進行異教祭祀，崇拜其異教神祇。例如莫德維尼亞人，散居於阿斯特拉罕地區、伏爾加河上游流域，最近由馬依諾夫先生在一八八九年《芬烏戈爾學會期刊》第五卷中所述。又如厄茲亞尼亞人，為莫德維尼亞人之一支，其祈禱文、謎語及咒文已由帕亞索寧先生在同一期刊一八九四年第十二卷中研究。再如沃特雅克人，其民間及聖詩標本已由伊爾約‧維希曼先生在一八九三年第十一卷中蒐集並出版。這些沃特雅克人主要分布於維亞特卡、喀山及彼爾姆等行政區。
在這樣的民族中，如果有任何機會，我們或許可以希冀學習到祭祀行為與祭祀詩歌真正起源，以及兩者相互關係的某些知識。我引述的權威人士全為芬蘭—烏戈爾學者，在他們手中，我們可以放心地避免那些在旅行者與傳教士著作中司空見慣的錯誤與誤解，即使這些人在同一地點駐守多年亦然。無論如何，人們將看到我並未忽視對附帶資料的研究。
證據，即使取自非雅利安人種，只要我們所遵循的權威是可信的，並由芬蘭烏戈爾學會（Societe Finno-Ougrienne）這樣傑出的學術機構所保證，亦無不可。
我們先前發現，這些芬蘭‧烏戈爾民族的神話學與吠陀的神話學呈現出令人矚目的類比，因此有可能他們的祭祀習俗也會展現出一些有用的平行現象，足以對照早期吠陀祭司的儀式。
